question.text: A farmer wants to lock in the price of wheat he will harvest in 3 months. He enters into a derivative contract to sell wheat at a predetermined price. What type of market participant is the farmer?

explanation.text: **The Logic:** The farmer is a hedger because he is using derivatives to reduce his exposure to price risk. Hedgers have an existing position in the underlying asset and use derivatives to protect against adverse price movements. **The Trap:** A speculator would not have an existing position in wheat — they would trade purely to profit from price movements. An arbitrageur exploits price differences across markets. A market maker provides liquidity but does not specifically seek to hedge. **Cross-Exam Context:** Hedging is a core concept tested in all NISM derivative exams (I, IV, VIII, XIII, XVI). **Key Takeaway:** Hedger = has existing exposure to the underlying + uses derivatives to REDUCE risk. The farmer's risk is falling wheat prices; selling futures/forwards locks in the price.
